records.ety: [L. corruptus, p. p. of corrumpere to corrupt; cor- + rumpere to break. See Rupture.]

defs: Changed from a sound to a putrid state; spoiled; tainted; vitiated; unsound.
quotes: Who with such corrupt and pestilent bread would feed them.

defs: Changed from a state of uprightness, correctness, truth, etc., to a worse state; vitiated; depraved; debased; perverted; as, corrupt language; corrupt judges.
quotes: At what ease Might corrupt minds procure knaves as corrupt To swear against you.

defs: Abounding in errors; not genuine or correct; as, the text of the manuscript is corrupt.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Corrupted; p. pr. & vb. n. Corrupting.]

defs: To change from a sound to a putrid or putrescent state; to make putrid; to putrefy.
To change from good to bad; to vitiate; to deprave; to pervert; to debase; to defile.
To draw aside from the path of rectitude and duty; as, to corrupt a judge by a bribe.
quotes: Heaven is above all yet; there sits a Judge That no king can corrupt.

defs: To debase or render impure by alterations or innovations; to falsify; as, to corrupt language; to corrupt the sacred text.
quotes: He that makes an ill use of it [language], though he does not corrupt the fountains of knowledge, . . . yet he stops the pines.

defs: To waste, spoil, or consume; to make worthless.
quotes: Lay not up for yourselves treasures upon earth, where moth and rust doth corrupt.

defs: To become putrid or tainted; to putrefy; to rot.
To become vitiated; to lose purity or goodness.
